coredata.dc:title: Solvent-free rapid synthesis of carbon nanodots at atmospheric pressure: Preparation of transparent light conversion films with ultrahigh efficient UV and blue light shielding
coredata.dc:creator: Gong, Xiao
Xu, Qingqing
Chen, Xiaona
Meng, Fanchao
Wang, Hangxiang
coredata.dc:description: In this age of technology, people are inevitably surrounded by electronic devices and light-emitting devices, but the radiation generated by these devices is very harmful to humans. Therefore, it is crucial to develop a stable absorbing material with efficient absorption capacity for UV and high-energy blue light (HEBL). In this paper, we report that carbon nanodots (CDs) with strong absorption peaks in the UV and HEBL can be rapidly synthesized in large scale by simply heating urea and citric acid at high temperature under atmospheric pressure without using any solvents. Interestingly, the large-scale prepared CDs possess green fluorescence emission upon excitation in both the UV and HEBL regions, exhibiting good photoconversion ability. Furthermore, ultrahigh efficient UV and HEBL shielding films can be prepared by embedding the CDs in a carboxymethyl cellulose matrix. By characterizing their transmission and absorption spectra, it can be found that the cellulose films containing 0.2 wt% CDs achieve almost complete shielding of UV-A, UV-B, and HEBL with a guaranteed visible light (550 nm) transmission rate of 80% or above. Finally, this cellulose film was further attached to a light-emitting diode (LED) chip to investigate its practical use in the field of UV and strong blue light shielding.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation and characterization of the CDs
Preparation and characterization of CDs@cel film

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of CDs and CDs@cel film. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Morphological and Structural characterizations of CDs. a) TEM and HRTEM (inset) images of CDs. b) XRD spectrum of CDs. c) Raman spectrum of CDs. d) FT-IR spectrum of CDs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
a) XPS spectrum. High-resolution XPS b) C 1s, c) N 1s and d) O 1s spectra of the CDs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Optical characterizations of CDs (0.0125 wt%). a) UV absorption, excitation and emission spectra of emission CDs. b) PL spectra of CDs under different excitations. c) PL spectra of CDs prepared at different reaction times at optimal excitation. d) PL spectra of CDs prepared at different reaction temperatures at optimal excitation. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Morphological and structural characterizations of CDs@cel film. a) Flexibility of the CDs@cel3 film under sunlight and UV light. b) and c) Cross-sectional and surface SEM images of CDs@cel3 film. d) Schematic representation of H-bonding between CDs and cellulose. e) FT-IR spectra of Cel and CDs@cel3 film. f) Stretching curve spectra of Cel and CDs@cel3 film. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Optical characterizations of CDs@cel film. a) Excitation and emission spectra of emission CDs@cel3 film. b) PL spectra of CDs@cel3 film under different excitations. c) PL spectra at optimal excitation of CDs@cel1, CDs@cel2, CDs@cel3, CDs@cel4 film. d) Room-temperature PL decays of CDs and CDs@cel3. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Shielding performance of CDs@cel film. a) and b) Transmittance and UV−VIS spectra of Cel, CDs@cel1, CDs@cel2, CDs@cel3, CDs@cel4 film. c) UV-A, UV-B, and HEBL-blocking activity of Cel, CDs@cel1, CDs@cel2, CDs@cel3, CDs@cel4 film. d) UV irradiation stability of CDs@cel3 film. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

a) EL spectra of 365 nm LED chip and chips attached by CDs@cel film (10 wt%). (inset: Optical photos of 365 nm LED chip and chips attached by CDs@cel film (10 wt%)). b) EL spectra of 430 nm LED chip and chips attached by CDs@cel film (10 wt%). (inset: Optical photos of 430 nm LED chip and chips attached by CDs@cel film (10 wt%)). c) and d) Optical photos and EL spectra of 450 nm LED chip and chips attached by different concentrations of CDs@cel film. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

head.ce:title: Solvent-free rapid synthesis of carbon nanodots at atmospheric pressure: Preparation of transparent light conversion films with ultrahigh efficient UV and blue light shielding

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: NHC Key Laboratory of Combined Multi-Organ Transplantation
Research Center for Diagnosis and Treatment of Hepatobiliary Diseases

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this age of technology, people are inevitably surrounded by electronic devices and light-emitting devices, but the radiation generated by these devices is very harmful to humans. Therefore, it is crucial to develop a stable absorbing material with efficient absorption capacity for UV and high-energy blue light (HEBL). In this paper, we report that carbon nanodots (CDs) with strong absorption peaks in the UV and HEBL can be rapidly synthesized in large scale by simply heating urea and citric acid at high temperature under atmospheric pressure without using any solvents. Interestingly, the large-scale prepared CDs possess green fluorescence emission upon excitation in both the UV and HEBL regions, exhibiting good photoconversion ability. Furthermore, ultrahigh efficient UV and HEBL shielding films can be prepared by embedding the CDs in a carboxymethyl cellulose matrix. By characterizing their transmission and absorption spectra, it can be found that the cellulose films containing 0.2 wt% CDs achieve almost complete shielding of UV-A, UV-B, and HEBL with a guaranteed visible light (550 nm) transmission rate of 80% or above. Finally, this cellulose film was further attached to a light-emitting diode (LED) chip to investigate its practical use in the field of UV and strong blue light shielding.

ce:section.ce:para: Sunlight, the main source of energy of the earth, is the basis and guarantee for the survival of all living things [1,2]. The spectrum of sunlight mainly consists of three wavelengths: ultraviolet (UV), visible (VIS), and infrared (IR), whose energy accounts for about ∼5%, ∼43%, and ∼52% of the total energy of sunlight, respectively [3]. Most UV-C (200–275 nm) and a certain amount of UV-B (275–320 nm) light can be absorbed by the ozone layer, while the remaining UV light reaches the earth's surface [4]. With the increase in environmental pollution and the dramatic increase in CO2 emissions, the ozone layer has been severely damaged, causing an increase in UV radiation reaching the earth's surface [5]. Short-wave UV-B radiation may cause metabolic chaos in the organism, accelerating skin aging, etc., while long-wave low-energy UV-A (320–400 nm) radiation may reduce human immunity more serious cases may cause damage to the DNA of the organism [6–8]. In addition, for certain UV-photosensitive products (e.g., photosensitive organics, foods, pharmaceuticals, etc.), strong UV exposure can cause changes in their properties that can be irreversibly harmful [9,10].

In addition to UV light, the high-energy blue light (HEBL, 400–450 nm) of visible light can also cause serious damage to humans [11]. Human life is currently surrounded by a variety of electronic devices (smartphones, tablets, and flat panel TVs using backlight technology, etc.), compact fluorescent lamps (CFL), and light-emitting diodes (LEDs) [12]. These devices emit intense blue light and small amounts of UV light, exposing people to more damaging radiation than ever before. Although natural blue light can help regulate the sleep schedule of organisms, improving alertness and relieving psychological stress, prolonged exposure to strong blue light may disrupt sleep order and consequently many health problems, such as depression, anxiety, heart disease, etc. Most importantly, strong blue light can penetrate the lens to damage the retina of the eye, and in more serious cases, cataracts and macular degeneration may occur [13,14].

As a green and sustainable polysaccharide-based polymer, functionalized cellulose films have received widespread attention [15,16]. Although cellulose films have good mechanical properties and transparency, they do not have light shielding capability [17]. Currently, the reported methods to achieve light shielding rely mainly on the incorporation of absorbers into the cellulose matrix [18]. Sun et al. prepared functionalized and enhanced carboxymethyl cellulose nanocomposite bioplastics for UV and blue light shielding using nano-metal organic framework as dopants [19]. Zhang et al. reported the UV shielding properties of composite films synthesized from graphene oxide (GO) nanosheets and regenerated cellulose [15]. Huang et al. chose aromatic polyamide nanofibers as additives to improve the mechanical and UV shielding properties of hydroxyethyl cellulose (HEC) membranes [18]. The improved blue light resistance of cellulose films are still limited compared to UV blocking materials [20–22]. This is mainly because most absorbers are difficult to achieve strong absorption peaks in both the UV region and the HEBL region. At the same time, their performance has many shortcomings in practical applications: (1) the films cannot effectively block short-wave blue light; (2) poor stability of the films used in applications; (3) the visible light transmission of the films is very low; and (4) the performance of the films used cannot meet the requirements. Currently, overconsumption of electronic products is a common phenomenon in the current information age, and it is critical to design and synthesize emerging short-wave blue light-resistant films to meet the above needs.

As a new type of fluorescent nanomaterial, Carbon dots (CDs) have adjustable fluorescence characteristics, high quantum yield, and excellent stability [23–25]. Moreover, due to the dual action of graphite C structure and surface groups, CDs may have absorption peaks in both UV and visible regions [26]. Based on their excellent performance, carbon quantum dots have been applied to luminescent solar concentrators [27–29], LED [30,31], UV shielding [32], and other fields [33–35]. However, due to the high temperature and high-pressure preparation method of carbon quantum dots reported so far, the synthesis conditions are complex, the chemical yield is low, and the reaction procedure is long, which limits and hinders the practical application of carbon quantum dots [36,37]. Due to its low energy consumption and relatively high yield, one-step pyrolysis is generally considered to be the most promising method for large-scale CDs preparation. For example, Rong et al. [38] prepared blue fluorescent N-CDs using guanidine chloride and citric acid as carbon sources by solid phase pyrolysis under nitrogen atmosphere, with a yield of about 20%. Li et al. [39] reported a CDs with a yield of about 69% obtained by direct pyrolysis of citric acid and ethylenediamine for selective detection of Fe3+.

In this study, CDs were directly large-scale synthesized using inexpensive small molecule materials (citric acid, urea) as the carbon source under 180 °C and atmospheric pressure reaction conditions within 60 min. In this reaction system, urea will decompose into small molecular substances and water at high temperature, thus assuming the role of nitrogen source and solvent at the same time, while citric acid acts as the carbon source for the formation of CDs. Accompanied by intense magnetic agitation, the prepared CDs have better size uniformity and higher yield. At the same time, due to the synergistic effect of carbon core and its surface functional groups containing N and O, our CDs have a wide range of absorption peaks in UV and HEBL, and the composite film prepared by embedding the CDs in the biodegradable cellulose film has excellent UV and strong blue light shielding effect.
Citric acid (CA) and urea were purchased from Aladdin. Carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC), ethanol, and others were purchased from Sinopharm.

Urea (40 mmol) and CA (1 mmol) were added to a 20 mL single-mouth flask and heated in an oil bath at 180 °C with magnetic stirring at 1000 rpm for 60 min. The single-mouth flask was cooled to room temperature, 20 mL of deionized water was added, and the mixed solution was sonicated in an ultrasonicator at 800 W for 20 min. Subsequently, after centrifugation at 5000 rpm for 8 min, the larger particles were removed through a 0.2 μm syringe filter to obtain a black-brown solution. The solution was dialyzed with ultrapure water for 24 h using an osmosis membrane (molecular weight cutoff of 0.5–1 kDa) to remove unreacted small molecules or impurities. Then, the dialyzed solution was dried at 80 °C for 12 h to obtain black CDs powder.

In the experiments, 0, 0.25, 0.5, 0.75, and 1 g black CDs powder was dissolved in 5 mL deionized water respectively to obtain dark yellow CDs solutions with different CDs contents. Carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC, 2 g) was added to 50 mL deionized water, stirred, and heated at 80 °C for 2 h to obtain a transparent cellulose solution. Then, the dark yellow solution with the same volume (50 μL) and different CDs content was added to the cellulose solution with the same mass (5 g) to get the mixed solution. The mass fraction of CDs in the mixed solution is about 0, 0.05, 0.1, 0.15, and 0.2 wt%, respectively. After ultrasonic mixing was uniform, the mixed solution was placed in the culture dish and dried in the oven at 80 °C for 4 h. The films with CDs mass fraction of 0, 0.05, 0.1, 0.15, and 0.2 wt% in the mixed solution before drying were named Cel, CDs@cel1, CDs@cel2, and CDs@cel3, CDs@cel4 respectively and were characterized separately.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images were registered by JEM-2100F TEM to characterize the distribution, size, and structure of CDs. X-ray diffraction spectra (XRD) of CDs were characterized on a D8 Advance diffractometer (Bruker, Germany, Kα = 1.5406 Å). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra of CDs were measured by using Nicolet 6700 FT-IR spectrometer (USA). Raman spectra were obtained in LABHRev-UV under 785 nm excitation. X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were taken by using a Thermo Fisher Scientific ESCALAB 250Xi spectrometer (USA). The photoluminescence (PL) spectra of the samples were recorded using a time-resolved fluorescence spectrometer (FL3-22, Jobin-Yvon, USA) at room temperature. The absorption and transmission spectra of the samples were collected by a UV2600 UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Japan). The tensile curves of the samples were tested on an electronic universal testing machine (Instron 5967).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of the CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1 illustrates the synthesis process of the CDs. Unlike the previously reported reaction conditions of high temperature and high pressure and organic solvents used in the preparation of CDs [40,41], CDs powders with excellent water solubility were prepared using inexpensive small molecules (CA, urea) as the carbon source under high temperature, atmospheric pressure, and solvent-free synthesis conditions. Meanwhile, the photoluminescent quantum yield (PLQY) of prepared CDs in dimethyl sulfoxide is 51.6%. Under atmospheric pressure, as urea has poor thermal stability, high temperature prompts the decomposition of urea molecules to produce small molecules and water, which makes urea molecules assume the role of nitrogen source and solvent at the same time. Meanwhile, the carbonization of urea and CA is more uniform and rapid under the conditions of vigorous magnetic stirring. Therefore, the CDs prepared by this method have better size uniformity and higher chemical yields.

The microscopic morphology, size distribution, and dispersion of the synthesized CDs were observed by the TEM images. As shown in Fig. 2 a, the CDs are nearly spherical in shape and show good dispersion and uniform size distribution. 200 points in the TEM images are analyzed for size statistics, and it is obvious that the average particle size of CDs is about 2.8 ± 0.05 nm (Fig. S1). In addition, the inset of Fig. 2a shows the HRTEM image of the CDs, and it can be observed that CDs have clear lattice stripes with 0.21 nm crystal plane spacing, which corresponds to the (100) crystal plane of graphite [42]. This phenomenon indicates that the CDs have some graphite-like crystal structure. Fig. 2b demonstrates the XRD pattern of CDs with 23° and 42° amorphous diffraction peaks corresponding to the (002) and (101) crystal planes of graphite, respectively, which further proves the presence of graphitic structure in the CDs, consistent with the result shown in the HRTEM image of the CDs [43]. The Raman spectrum of CDs is shown in Fig. 2c. There are two characteristic peaks at 1330 and 1585 cm−1, corresponding to the disordered D band and the graphite G band respectively. Through calculation, the IG/ID intensity ratio is about 1.3, which further shows that CDs has good crystallinity [34].

Chemical structure of the CDs was investigated using FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 2d). The broad peaks in the FT-IR pattern between 2700–3600 cm−1 may be attributed to the stretching vibrations of –OH and –NH2, while the absorption peaks at 1623, 1458, 1342, and 1066 cm−1, respectively, match with the stretching vibration of CO, stretching vibration of CC, stretching vibration of aromatic C-N, and stretching vibration of C-O [44]. The above results suggest that a good deal of amino and carboxyl functional groups may exist on surface of the CDs, which indicates that the CDs have excellent hydrophilic properties. To verify the above results, the compositional components of the CDs were analyzed using XPS. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the complete XPS spectrum showed three peaks at 284.4, 399.9, and 532.2 eV, indicating that the CDs consisted of C, N, and O elements with calculated atomic ratios of 55.01%, 25.7%, and 19.2%, respectively. The possible functional group components in the CDs were investigated by high-resolution fitting analysis of the three elements C, N, and O. In Fig. 3b, the high-resolution spectrum of C 1s is separated into four peaks at 284.3, 285.3, 287.5, and 288.7 eV corresponding to CC/C-C, C-N, CO, and O-CO, respectively [45–47], while the N 1s (Fig. 3c) showed three peaks at 398.9, 399.9 and 400.7 eV, corresponding to amino-N, and pyrrolic N and graphite N groups [48,49]. In addition, the high-resolution spectrum of O 1s in Fig. 3d presents two peaks at 531.6 and 532.7 eV corresponding to OC and O-C/O-H, respectively [50]. These three high-resolution spectra together indicate that the CDs structure has the presence of abundant carboxyl and amino groups [51]. The presence of these functional groups makes CDs have excellent water solubility, which facilitates the formation of uniform and stable composite cellulose films by forming hydrogen bonds between CDs and the groups on the surface of CMC [52].

As shown in Fig. 4 a, the entire absorption spectrum of CDs extends from the UV region to HEBL of visible light and consists of three main components. The shoulder peak at 280 nm can be attributed to the π-π* leap of CC, the strong absorption peak at 328 nm is caused by the n-π* leap of CO and C-N, and the absorption peak at 408 nm may be associated with the presence of abundant functional groups on the surface of CDs [44]. Combined with the results of FT-IR and XPS analysis, it may be due to the synergistic effect of the carbon nuclei and the surface N- and O-containing functional groups of the CDs resulting in the broad absorption peaks of the CDs, which implies that the prepared CDs have a strong ability to absorb UV and HEBL. In addition, another pleasing aspect is that the absorption peak of CDs is cut off near 450 nm, which is exactly for the strong blue region of visible light. This property is a prerequisite for the CDs as an absorber to guarantee the visible light transmission rate. Fig. 4b demonstrates the changes of the emission spectra of the CDs solution when the excitation wavelength is changed from 370 nm to 480 nm. When the excitation wavelength is changed from 370 nm to 440 nm, the PL intensity of CDs gradually increases but there is no change in the maximum emission peak position, while there is a slight red shift in the emission peak position of CDs when the excitation wavelength is changed from 440 nm to 480 nm. This may be due to the presence of different types of surface groups on the graphitized structural surface of CDs allowing different surface and edge states between the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) bands [53]. In addition, to investigate the effect of reaction conditions on the optical properties of CDs, the PL spectra of the prepared CDs under different reaction conditions were tested separately. In addition, in order to further explore the influence of reaction conditions on the optical properties of CDs, the absorption, PLE, and PL spectra of CDs prepared under different reaction conditions were tested. As shown in Fig. 4c and d, the fluorescence of CDs under optimal excitation exhibits a blue-green light change with increasing reaction temperature/increasing reaction time. When the reaction was carried out at 140 °C for 1 h or 180 °C for 10 min, the CDs showed blue fluorescence, while further increasing the temperature or prolonging the reaction time resulted in green fluorescence. The formation mechanism of CDs was further explored by testing the absorption and PLE spectra of CDs under different reaction conditions. As shown in Figs. S2a and b, at low temperature or for a short time, CDs only has a separate absorption peak near 330 nm, while with the increase of temperature or the extension of time, CDs appear a new absorption peak near 410 nm. A similar phenomenon was observed in the PLE spectra of CDs corresponding to the absorption peaks (Figs. S2c and d). According to the mechanism provided in the literature, this phenomenon can be explained as follows: at low temperatures or in a short time, the decomposition of urea molecules is weak, and the small molecule polymer with blue fluorescence emission is formed by the strong hydrogen bond between urea and citric acid. However, with the increase in temperature or the extension of time, urea molecules decompose to produce isocyanate, which has a strong reaction ability with citric acid and rapidly dehydrates and condenses to form CDs with the green fluorescence emission [44]. To further investigate the PL spectra and photoconversion ability of CDs under different excitations, the PL spectra of CDs prepared under different reaction conditions at 365 nm excitation were tested. The results in Figs. S2e and f show that CDs exhibits the same fluorescence change condition under 365 nm excitation, which is consistent with the PL spectra of CDs under optimal excitation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of CDs@cel film
ce:section.ce:para: As a natural solid substrate, CMC has the advantages of non-toxic, low-price, biodegradable, and water-soluble, which makes it an excellent carrier for fixing CDs. As shown in Fig. S3, natural CMC film has a high degree of transparency. As a load of CDs increases, CDs@cel film gradually turns pale yellow. In Fig. 5 a, it can be clearly observed that the cellulose film has excellent transparency and flexibility. To study the morphological structure of CDs@cel film, its cross section and surface were characterized by the SEM images. Fig. 5b and c shows that the CDs@cel film is about 35 μm thickness and highly dense, and has a smooth surface at the nanoscale. The combination of CMC and CDs can be more visually recognized by the schematic diagram shown in Fig. 5d. The structure of CDs@cel film in the schematic was demonstrated using the FT-IR spectra of Cel and CDs@cel film. As shown in Fig. 5e, the broad absorption peak at 3350 cm−1 in the FT-IR spectrum of Cel is due to the stretching vibration of –OH. The strong absorption peaks of 1590 cm−1, 1409 cm−1, and 1060 cm−1 may be attributed to antisymmetric stretching vibration of carboxylate, symmetric stretching vibration, and C-O-C stretching vibration, respectively. Compared with Cel, the FTIR spectrum of CDs@cel film shows new absorption peaks at 1695 cm−1 and 1465 cm−1, which is due to the presence of CDs. In addition, in terms of the intensity of the absorption peak, CDs@cel film becomes weaker at 3350 cm−1 and stronger at 1405 cm−1, which indicates that the content of hydroxyl group in CDs@cel film decreases and the content of carboxyl group increases. Interestingly, according to previous reports, the intensity of the infrared vibration band may be influenced by both the dipole moment and the energy transition [54,55]. However, whether the energy transition exists depends on the properties of the functional groups. For a specific functional group, the intensity of infrared absorption peak is affected by multiple factors such as hydrogen bond, conjugate group, Fermi resonance, solvent effect, and several functional groups [56]. For this system, compared with pure cellulose film, the infrared spectrum of CDs@cel film has the characteristic peaks of Cel and CDs, which can indicate that there is no conjugated group and Fermi resonance in this system. In addition, there is no other difference between pure cellulose film and CDs@cel film in the preparation process except addition of the CDs. And the CDs have been purified, washed, and dried before use, so the interference of solvent effect can be fully eliminated. In summary, it means that the changes in infrared spectral intensity can be attributed to the existence of hydrogen bonds. This further indicates that the CDs have a good bond with cellulose. It is the existence of hydrogen bonds between the hydroxyl groups possessed by CMC and the surface functional groups of CDs that makes CDs@cel film have a stable structure on the one hand and prevents the aggregation of CDs on the other hand while improving the strength and toughness of CMC. Good mechanical properties give CDs@cel film more practical value. Therefore, by comparing the stress-strain curves of CDs@cel film and Cel, it can be seen that the tensile strength of cellulose film after the addition of CDs is further increased from the original 28.4 MPa to 37.0 MPa (Fig. 5f). Compared with the tensile strength, it is worth paying more attention to the change of the film's elongation. The elongation of CDs@cel film was 39.7%, while the elongation of Cel was only 8%. This may be due to the hydrogen bonds between CDs and the cellulose matrix, which consume part of the energy to preferentially break during stretching and thus improve the mechanical properties of the film [57]. This data indicates that the plasticity of the film was significantly increased after the addition of CDs. For practical applications, better plasticity means that the film can be shaped into a variety of shapes according to the occasions it is used.

The PL excitation and emission spectra of CDs@cel film were tested to explain the photoconversion ability of CDs@cel film (Fig. 6 a), with the emission peak of CDs@cel film around 510 nm, corresponding to the green light region of visible light. As shown in Fig. 6b, the excitation-dependent spectra of the films further indicate that there is no significant shift in the position of the emission peaks under various excitations. This may be because the hydrogen bond formed between CDs and cellulose in the film inhibits the action of some surface states, resulting in a more stable fluorescence emission of CDs@cel compared with CDs [53]. In addition, CDs solution and CDs@cel film fluorescence and excitation-dependent spectra at different CDs loadings were tested, which can be used to evaluate and compare the optical properties of CDs and CDs@cel, and also helps to explain the UV and intense blue light absorption ability of the films. As shown in Fig. S4, with the increase of CDs concentration, the optimal excitation of the PL spectrum and emission peak of the CDs solution both produce a certain degree of redshift, which may be attributed to the accumulation of CDs to a certain degree. Similar to the test results of the CDs solution, the PL spectrum of CDs@cel thin film also has a redshift phenomenon (Fig. 6c). In order to characterize the optical conversion ability of CDs@cel, 300 nm, 380 nm, and 420 nm were selected as excitation light sources, and the PL spectra of CDs@cel films with different concentrations were tested respectively (Fig. S5). It can be obviously observed that CDs@cel films all present green fluorescence emission at 510 nm under different excitation. This indicates that CDs@cel film has excellent UV and strong blue light conversion ability. The average fluorescence lifetime of the CDs changed from 5.86 ns to 11.01 ns after embedded in cellulose film. This may be because, in solution, the excited state electrons of the CDs return to the ground state mainly through a fast nonradiative leap process, leading to a rapid decay of the fluorescence lifetime. However, in CDs@cel film, the chemical bond formed between CDs and the substrate may reduce the surface defects of CDs and provide a more efficient pathway for a radiative leap. Therefore, the lifetime of the film is longer than that of the CDs in solution [58]. This conclusion was further verified by testing the absolute yield of CDs aqueous solution and CDs@cel, which showed that the PLQY of CDs aqueous solution was 9.85%, while the PLQY of CDs@cel obtained when CDs was compounded with cellulose was 20.74%.

The shielding and absorption ability of CDs@cel films for UV and strong blue light can be observed by the absorption and transmission spectra of different loadings of CDs@cel (Fig. 7 a and b). It can be distinctly noticed from the figure that the transmission rate of CDs@cel in the full UV and strong blue light bands gradually decreases with the increase of CDs concentration. When the concentration of CDs is 0.2 wt%, the cellulose film achieves the full UV and strong blue light shielding with almost no effect on the visible light. Thus, the full wavelength UV and strong blue light shielding can be achieved by the CDs@cel4 film. The transmission rates of different CDs@cel in visible light (550 nm) and UV-A, UV-B, and HEBL were compared (Fig. 7c). CDs@cel3 film blocked 93% of UV-A, 95% of UV-B, and 97% of visible light (540 nm). CDs@cel4 film perfectly blocked 99% of UV-A, UV-B, and HEBL, while having 79% transparency to visible light. These films exhibited sufficiently high transparency to visible light while blocked most of the harmful UV and HEBL. For practical applications, the stability of the UV absorption properties of CDs@cel films is critical for their long-term use. Therefore, we placed CDs@cel film under long-term constant irradiation with a 85 W UV light source and measured it periodically by UV–visible transmission spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 7d, CDs@cel film consistently maintained excellent UV and strong blue light shielding during 24 h of continuous UV light irradiation.

The UV and strong blue light blocking performance of CDs@cel film was confirmed by attaching CDs@cel film to LED chips of different wavelengths. For the UV region and the strong blue region, LED chips with 365 nm and 430 nm were selected as the experimental light, respectively. As shown in the spectra of Fig. 8 a and b, the original chip light at 365 nm and 430 nm disappears while the green fluorescence peak of the CDs appears after the attachment of CDs@cel film. The change in fluorescence color can also be clearly observed in the optical pictures shown in the insets. For the end of the HEBL at 450 nm, the 450 nm LED chip was selected as the characteristic light because it is in the trailing stage of the CDs, and the effect of the CDs concentration change on its blue light shielding was investigated. As the concentration of CDs increases, the strong blue light emitted by the LED chip is compounded with the green fluorescence emitted by the CDs, making the compound fluorescence show a change from blue to green (Fig. 8c). The change of EL spectra of LED chip further confirmed the effect of CDs on blue shielding of LED. As shown in Fig. 8d, the intensity of the emission peak at 450 nm gradually decreased with the increasing concentration of CDs, and the fluorescence at 450 nm completely disappeared when the concentration of CDs was 10.0 wt%. It can be seen that the prepared CDs are excellent UV and strong blue light absorbers. In addition, to evaluate the stability of CDs@cel film, the effect of driving current change on the PL emission spectra was investigated. When the driving current increased from 20 mA to 100 mA (Fig. S6), the PL emission peaks excited by the three LED chips at 365 nm, 430 nm, and 450 nm did not show significant changes. This phenomenon indicates that CDs@cel film has high photostability and thermal stability over a wide range of driving currents. At the same time, due to the concentration-dependent property of the CDs to a certain extent, the emission peak is located near 550 nm at this time and is yellow-green. Therefore, it can be concluded that the prepared CDs@cel film has excellent fluorescence conversion properties and can efficiently shield UV and strong blue light.

In summary, in this study, CDs with good size uniformity and high chemical yield were rapidly prepared by using inexpensive small molecules as carbon sources and taking advantage of the thermal decomposition of urea at high-temperature and atmospheric pressure without solvent. More importantly, the prepared CDs had strong absorption peaks in the UV and HEBLs and produced green fluorescence emission upon excitation in both the UV and HEBLs with good photoconversion ability. Meanwhile, the UV and strong blue light shielding films were prepared by embedding the CDs in a CMC matrix. Through the characterization of their transmission and absorption spectra, it can be found that the CDs could achieve almost complete shielding of UV-A, UV-B, and HEBL at a concentration of 0.2 wt% with a guaranteed visible light (550 nm) transmission rate of 80% or above. Finally, the practical usability of this cellulose film for UV and HEBL shielding was investigated by attaching it to an LED chip.

Xiao Gong: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Formal analysis, Funding acquisition, Project administration. Qingqing Xu: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Synthesis, Characterization, Writing – original manuscript, review & editing, Visualization, Formal analysis. Xiaona Chen: Writing – review & editing, Characterization, review & editing. Fanchao Meng: Writing – review & editing, Characterization, review & editing. Hangxiang Wang: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing, review & editing, Funding acquisition, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21774098) and Research Project of Jinan Microecological Biomedicine Shandong Laboratory (JNL-2022010B).
